As much as I like Cookie Clicker, I unfortunately have to give it a negative review.

I have played far more than my Steam playtime suggests, including the browser version, the mobile version and the Switch version. Cookie Clicker is undeniably one of the most important idle games ever made. It basically created or at least massively shaped the entire idle/clicker genre, and very few games have been as influential.

However, when you compare Cookie Clicker to modern idle game standards, I honestly do not think it holds up that well anymore.

Everything before the first Ascension is great. You make steady progress, unlock lots of fun achievements like True Neverclick, Hardcore or selling a grandma, and the game constantly feels fresh and creative. Clicking a Golden Cookie feels exciting because it gives you a meaningful boost, and pulling off your first combos is genuinely fun.

But this is also where the main problem begins.

At a certain point, especially after the second or third Ascension, meaningful progress becomes almost completely dependent on Golden Cookie combos. The game slowly changes from:

idling, buying upgrades, clicking Golden Cookies, getting boosts and having fun

into:

sitting in front of the screen for hours, waiting and hoping that Golden Cookies give you the right effects in the right order.

Cookie Clicker suffers heavily from poor late-game balancing, and this becomes very noticeable after a few Ascensions. Progress no longer feels like a satisfying idle loop, but more like waiting for RNG to allow you to actually play the game.

Another issue is Sugar Lumps. Being limited to roughly one Sugar Lump per day is just too extreme. Sure, they do not give an absurd CPS boost by themselves, but if you want all buildings at level 10 for the achievements, you are looking at around three years of simply waiting. That is not engaging long-term progression; it is just time-gating.

On top of that, some achievements are simply excessive, such as clicking 27,777 Golden Cookies. At that point it does not feel challenging anymore, just unnecessarily tedious.

Cookie Clicker starts out brilliantly and absolutely deserves respect for its influence on the genre. But after the early and mid game, it loses a lot of what makes it fun.

Sadly, because of the late-game balancing, extreme time-gating and overreliance on Golden Cookie RNG, I have to give it a negative review.

Who doesn't like cookies? This is an addictive idle game about making cookies and possibly discovering the secrets of the universe doing so as you go from baking cookies that not even the raccoons would eat to making the entire economy and reality revolve around your cookies. Also maybe start a grandma uprising for reasons we don't understand why. If you like idle games, this is one of the more recommended ones to get.

Cookie Clicker is an absolute OG. There’s honestly not much else that needs to be said. It’s simple, addictive, chaotic, and somehow impossible to stop playing once you get going (I say with my 9 minutes of playtime). Watching the numbers grow never stops being satisfying, and it’s one of those games that helped define the idle/clicker genre for so many people. A true classic.

I enjoyed this game. never have I been amazed at the sheer intricacies of cookies.
And for that. Here's a recipe from Betty Crocker:
1. Oven Prep 
Place your rack in the center of the oven; this is the best place to bake cookies because the heat circulates most evenly throughout the oven.

2. Mix Wet Ingredients 
To get the best softened butter, leave it on the counter until your finger leaves an imprint (30 minutes to 1 hour). Once softened (not melted), beat the butter and sugars into a light, fluffy mixture. It’s better to have your butter on the cooler side than overly softened so the cookies don’t spread too much. Then, beat in the egg and vanilla until completely blended.

3. Stir in Dry Ingredients
Stir in flour, baking soda, and salt until they just disappear into the dough. Finally, add the chocolate chips (and optional nuts) last to prevent overmixing, which can lead to tough cookies.

4. Scoop and Bake
Using a cookie scoop (or soup spoon), scoop the dough, forming about a 1 3/4-inch rounded dough blob. Bake one cookie sheet at a time. Check cookies after 8 minutes to see if they are golden brown around the edges and just shy of being completely baked in the center. If needed, bake a minute or two longer. Don’t overbake them if you want a soft, gooey texture.

5. Cool and Eat
Cool cookies on the cookie sheet for two minutes, then move them to a cooling rack. This allows them to set up a bit before removing, resulting in perfect chewy centers and crispy edges.

It's deceptively simple, but if you're gunning for all of the achievements like I am, you have to buckle down. Some of the end-game ones take many hours of grinding and a pretty deep knowledge of how to do combos and optimize your cookie output. That being said, it is enjoyable and the grinding feels worth it. I love Cookie Clicker, but I also hate Cookie Clicker.
